speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is August 6, 1996. The survivor is Otto Snyder, interviewer Merle Goldberg, city/state, Pasadena, California, United States of America. Language of the interview, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rob--

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name at birth, Schneider.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Merle Goldberg. Today is August 6th, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Otto Snyder in the English language in Pasadena, California. OK, Mr. Snyder, to begin with, can you tell me your full name as you are called today and spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm called Otto Snyder. O-T-T-O, S-N-Y-D-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and can you tell me the name you were born with at birth and spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Originally, I was called Otto Schneider-- S-C-H-N-E-I-D-E-R. I changed my name when I naturalized in the United States in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a middle name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Alfred.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that the name you were-- is that the name you were given as a baby in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got it, Alfred, at the same time as Otto, named after an uncle who has died shortly before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, now that you mention that, what if-- for whom are-- for whom are you named for your first name, if Alfred was for the uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It was standard at that time, the two names Otto and Ernst. And my older brother was Ernst.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to your brother though, we still have a couple-- a few questions I need to ask you about yourself. Can you tell me your full birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: August 2, 1910.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city and country were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna, at that time capital of the Austrian-Hungarian monarchy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while you were growing up, did-- did you have any nicknames or any terms of endearment anybody called you besides Otto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not when I was growing up. Later, when I was about 11, I joined the Boy Scouts, and they combined the Otto with the first letters of my family name, and I was generally called Schnotto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Schnotto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schnotto. Otto Schneider.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now who called you this name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just happened in the group. I don't know who brought it up the first time within the group of about 30 boys. That's what they dubbed me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, besides the boys from this group, was there anyone else who called you this name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Was generally-- I became known under that name. I didn't like it. I fought with people about it. But it stuck until very late in my life. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Since you were born in 1910, you're kind of a walking encyclopedia of the 20th century on some level. So-- but before we talk about that, I was wondering if you could tell me a little bit about your family. What I think I'm interested in most to find out in the beginning is that-- like lots of Austrian and German Jews that the families had been in whatever city they lived in for many hundreds of years. Do you know-- or can you tell me how long-- from each side, both your mother and your father's side-- your family-- families had been in the-- in Vienna or in the Austro-Hungarian empire?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's father-- so my grandfather, whom I never knew-- obtained citizenship in Vienna in 1867 after coming from Nikolsburg in Bohemia, then also part of the Austrian empire. But that's when he came and got his citizenship papers. Consequently, my own citizenship paper in Austria was also dated 1867 because it led back. But his family were in Vienna much earlier. He was married to a Viennese woman whose family had been in Vienna much, much longer. I don't know exactly where, but they, at that time, owned a big feed store in Vienna and were quite well known, and went back quite a number of years before. So I was told I am at least the fifth generation in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that family name? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- that family name, that was Pater-- P-A-T-E-R. They had a store in the 12th District in Vienna. Now, on my mother's side, the-- their name was Epstein-- E-P-S-T-E-I-N. Quite familiar and common. They also had been in Vienna for a long time, especially on the grandfather's side. I don't know exactly how long. The grandmother came also from Bohemia, from Rychnov, as it was called. Czech name was Rychnov nad Kneznou. [LAUGHS] And the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Grandmother was-- the grandma's name was Munk-- M-U-N-K. Maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, if you could tell me a little bit about your parents. If you could tell me their full names and where they were born, what they did, how they met, perhaps?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father first-- he was born July 29, 1874, in Vienna. He was the second-- or no, the third of a family of five-- five children-- or maybe seven. I'm not quite sure now. And he grew up in Vienna; spent all his life in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: He did three years mandatory military service from 1895 to 1898, then went out on his own, and eventually started his own business in Vienna. Maybe we stop there and continue? No, you should just-- you should keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't have to keep-- if you can keep going, so I don't have to keep asking you questions, that's really much more advantageous.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And in 1900 and-- end of 1906, he was invited by another gentleman, an older man, to come for a Sunday dinner in their house. That Sunday dinner was prepared by a young lady who was, at that time, to be married. That's the way he met my mother. It was a so-called spoken-to marriage. And it turned out that the two got together, and in 1907, April 15, they married, one day after my mother's 20th birthday. And Mother came from that family, Epstein, as I said before. And she had a very good education, finishing school. She was also trained as a dressmaker. She spoke French, English, Czech, and of course, German.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there's something you haven't told us about your mother, her name-- her full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. My mother's full name was Rosa-- she had no middle name-- Epstein. And eventually, of course, she became Rosa Schneider.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your father was in the military, what branch of the military did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was with the cavalry, in-- at that time, quite well known cavalry regiment, the dragoons. The regiment number was three. And they were mainly stationed in Krakow, in Poland, at that time part of the monarchy, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he later on told us many times it was probably the best time of his early life. He was freed from the restrictions of his Orthodox Jewish family. And of course, in the military, that wasn't observed. And I know when he came back from service, he was, well-- how do you say-- he was-- what's the word? Assimilated.

speaker: interviewee
text: From then on, he kept the High Holidays just to honor his mother who, till her last day, kept to the absolute Orthodox Jewish customs. But when he got married and moved away, we just didn't keep them anymore except the High Holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother and her family? How religious were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's family-- they were completely assimilated. They didn't keep anything, not even the High Holidays. And they just lived like all the other people, like the assimilated people. They were Austrians, actually. More Austrians than Jews. And that's the way my family-- that's the way I grow up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I know you really can't remember when you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: However, I was wondering if you could talk about your earliest memories. You just indicated that your family was assimilated, so I guess there wasn't a lot of Jewish life in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in my family, except occasionally, when we got in touch with Dad's mother. Dad's father had died in 1901, so they was long gone. And only occasionally, when we went to visit Grandmother, of course, we had to abide by her customs. And we did that to honor her. My earliest memory might be very funny. I was about three years old in kindergarten. And I remember one thing very vividly. I was wearing a red polo shirt. And the kindergarten teacher was calling for the custodian to come in and wipe up a puddle at my feet. And I was a very embarrassed little boy. But that's what had happened, and the custodian was used to it. I feel like I'm not the only one. That's my earliest memory. Next memory was-- Mother and I were standing in front of our store. We were two steps from the store entrance to the street level. She was standing on the upper step, and on the street was a soldier in uniform with the gun-- the-- at that time, the called it tornister on his back, and the side arm. He had pick me up and kissed me. I was very proud of it. And I couldn't understand why mother was wiping her tears. My dad had just been called up for services. It was September, 1914, the very first week of the First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, if anything, do you actually remember about the First World War? Living in Vienna, it was probably-- you were a little removed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a little-- I was not removed at all. I know we were-- we had our store and our sewing outfit, and we had government orders. And our sewing room was manufacturing soldiers' shirts that kept us going. We got the material from the-- from the army. And we were-- we had to cutter, and mother was supervising the sewing room. And I was playing around the sewing machines many time. Maybe a nuisance, but I was taken very nicely by the sewing people, the sewing operators.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your father's business, or your family's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it located, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was located in the sixth district of Vienna. It was Mariahilf. And the street name was Stumpergasse-- S-T-U-M-P-E-R, gasse-- number 62. Incidentally, further down in a number-- I think it was number 14 or something like that-- was also a house in which a former army member of the Austrian army by the name Adolf Hitler had-- had temporary lodgings. Yes, it was Adolf Hitler had lived in Stumpergasse in Vienna for a short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a child, or when-- were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- no, that was not while I was a child. It was maybe a little bit later. It might have been in the late teens or early 20s. I don't know exactly. But I remember it was-- became quite well known.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't tell us the name of the-- of your family's business. You told us the name of the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name-- the-- the company was known as Siegfried Schneider-- Siegfried was my dad's first name. Siegfried Schneider [NON-ENGLISH] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Shirt or underwear manufacturing company. And that was on the front of our business, going over the area of three windows, the complete front of the store, plus the sewing room which had windows to the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since your father served again in World War-- again, was he in the same unit? Or what-- where did he serve? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. While he was with the dragoons, he also had gotten training in communication, and he was a communication telegraph specialist. He had learned operating the Morse machines. And as specialist as such, although he was already 40 years old, he was called immediately when the First World War started because they needed the communication. But he then served also until the end of the war in 1918 in the infantry. He was wounded twice. During that time, he occasionally came back to Vienna. I have a very nice picture where he's seen in his uniform with his sergeants walking, the three stars on his collar, and surrounded by some other people of his outfit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] During the war-- at the beginning, it wasn't too bad. But by 1916, I remember, we started occasionally being hungry. I had an uncle-- my mother's oldest brother was also in the army, but he was a-- a lieutenant in the supply corps. So occasionally, he was able to slip a little bit food to us, occasionally. Didn't happen many times. I remember one day in 1916 that Mother and I, after closing the stores, walked a few blocks to a local grade school, stood in line with some other people, gradually inched forward, came to the entrance. There, we got a plate. We had brought along a spoon. And then as you got into the [INAUDIBLE] where two rows of dishes-- of tables with benches. And at the end, there were two ladies standing with huge cauldrons and ladled out corn mush-- polenta, as we called it at that time-- into the plates they had given us. Sprinkle a little sugar and cinnamon on it. We found a place on those long tables, and that was on-- our only hot meal for the day. It had gotten tight.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- what about any-- your brother? You didn't tell us about him.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was taken right at the beginning of the war to Budapest, where the older sister of my brother was married to the station master of the central railroad station in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, wait. We need to stop. I'm a little confused. You said the older sister of your brother. You--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [INAUDIBLE]. Older sister of my dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest sister of my dad, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have to tell us your brother's name as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My brother's name was Ernst, and he had a middle name Moritz after my dad's father. And when the war started, mother was left in Vienna with the little boy and the business. So aunt and uncle in Budapest took the older brother off my mother's hands, and he stayed with them until 1921. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't-- I hardly knew him. And in 1921, it was a little bit difficult time of [? reinteresting. ?] But in 1916, something else happened interesting. I remember one afternoon, I walked proudly along in front of the store, and I had a little rucksack with a slate board and a little sponge, and the little ink. I was ready for first grade. And I hoped everybody of the passengers saw me. After all, I was going to school the next day. And that day started something which was very influential in my life. That very first day, I found out that I was Jewish. That I was Jewish. What happened in Austria-- religious instruction was mandatory in schools up to age 18. I believe it still is now. But at that time-- the first grade-- the teacher told the whole class-- I think there were about 40 to 45 boys-- that the priest would come twice a week and instruct our boys in the catechism, the Christian Bible. And then she said, of course, I know there are some of you are Protestants and some are Jews. You will have to-- well, she didn't say Jews. That was not genteel enough. She called us Israelites. And you will have to go twice a week in the afternoon, after regular school hours, to a centralized district school where there would be a minister of the Protestants or a Jewish-- maybe a rabbi, or anyway, an instructor to instruct you in your religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then she asked, who are the Protestants? There were three boys who raised their hands. Two were Lutherans, and was-- one was a Calvinist. You had to go to different schools. And now where are the Israelites? No-- I had no idea what an Israelite was. Then she [INAUDIBLE] one or the other four. So she finally said, now, which one of you boys are not going to church on Sunday? Up went our five hands. So she gave us the written information to hand to our parents where we had to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the way home, we got beat up for the first time as Christ killers. Because that was a giveaway. The rest of the boys knew, these are the damn Jews. And actually, we were beaten up on our way home. That lasted for about a week. Our parents had to come pick us up. Usually was one of the parents picked up all the boys and got them out of the immediate school vicinity and distribute us to our homes. And so that was the first time that I found out that I'm different-- a Jew. I have encountered the same later on, occasionally, in my life. But by that time, I knew how to help myself. Sometimes with fists, sometimes with persuasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time in 1916, when the boys were beating you up, did you-- how did you retaliate, if at all? Or did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you fought back. You didn't submit. But after all, there were many of them and there were very few of us. So we probably ran. I don't quite remember. But we got home-- not bloodied, but beaten up, and a little bit afraid. And next morning, of course, getting to school, you dropped in single, was no problem. On the way home, there was already one of the mothers or one of the fathers picking us up and taking us home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What I'm going to ask you now has nothing to do with what you just told me. But I wanted to get the name of your uncle who worked-- who was in the-- in World War I who gave you some supplies from time to time. Who-- you didn't tell us his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Siegfried Epstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, when you talk about people, if you could-- like you would say my mother's brother or-- or if you're going to talk about someone. Could you please-- so we don't have to go back, and I have to ask you-- if you remember to tell us someone's full name as you speak to them rather-- and explain who they are in relationship to you, of course. But it's very helpful if we know the person's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You want the name of the family in Budapest where my brother spent his early years?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course, because it was the same name as my grandparents on my father's side-- his mother. Their name was Klamper. His name was Zigmund, with a Z-- Z-I-G-M-U-N-D, Klamper-- K-L-A-M-P-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, you don't have to spell everything-- because one of the reasons we don't like that is that it stops the flow of the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if there are any spelling questions, although--

speaker: interviewee
text: You ask him.

speaker: interviewer
text: I will ask you later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was married to my dad's older sister. Her name was Milly-- Emily.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you encountered this anti-Semitism in 1916, is it something that you-- once the parents had come to school and-- did you put it behind you? Or how did you deal? Do you-- do you remember how you even dealt with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not remember how I dealt with it then. But I remember that in fourth grade, the same school, we had a very anti-Semitic teacher in fourth grade who made remarks, and many times, disadvantaged the few Jewish boys in class. And when my dad found out about it-- I told him-- he was able to transfer me to a different school in the same district. And there was very lucky to get a-- a teacher who eventually became of my favorite teachers in that year-- in that school, in that grade. He also then prepared me for the entrance exams for the next school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the district in which you lived in Vienna-- how-- how Jewish was it? Or was it very assimilated?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- the district, as such, was average. But the street, Stumpergasse, was about 60% Jewish. There are a lot of Jewish stores and a lot of Jewish people, and there was even one Orthodox [INAUDIBLE] house, a [? prayer ?] house, like a private little synagogue, a storefront synagogue. And I remember those people on Saturday mornings. We saw the-- the Orthodox Jews flock to that little-- to that little house and had their-- their services there. We-- we did not belong. We belonged to a different temple-- belonged. We occasionally went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the synagogue that you occasionally went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the-- that particular one. But the one where we went-- of course, all the synagogues at that time were Orthodox. But the one I went to was in the same district in [NON-ENGLISH]. And there we occasionally, when we went, went there on the High Holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're gonna stop here and change the reel. OK?
